content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 31, 2017

Delphi Team
Attn: Peggy Bimbi
3310 Parker Hill Rd
Santa Rosa, CA 95404

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-004

Dear Ms. Bimbi:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding campaign provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

FACTS

You are preparing to start up a ballot measure committee. The ballot measure will be "No on
Measure ___" sponsored by medical marijuana patients, and legal medical marijuana growers and
manufacturers in the State of California. Although medical marijuana is legal in California, it is not
permitted under Federal law and as a result legal medical marijuana growers and manufacturers are
not allowed to open bank accounts. At this point, all of their business is cash based.

ANALYSIS

1. Can you advise if there is any other way that a business can legally give more than
$99.99 without having a checking account?

There is not. The Act prohibits making or receiving cash contributions of one hundred
dollars or more. Section 84300 provides:

"No contribution of one hundred dollars ($100) or more shall be made or
received in cash. A cash contribution shall not be deemed received if it is not
negotiated or deposited and is returned to the contributor before the closing date
of the campaign statement on which the contribution would otherwise be reported.
If a cash contribution, other than a late contribution, as defined in Section 82036,
is negotiated or deposited, it shall not be deemed received if it is refunded within
72 hours of receipt. In the case of a late contribution, as defined in Section 82036,

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-004
Page No. 2

it shall not be deemed received if it is returned to the contributor within 48 hours
of receipt."

"(b) No expenditure of one hundred dollars ($100) or more shall be made
in cash.

"(c) No contribution of one hundred dollars ($100) or more other than an
in-kind contribution shall be made unless in the form of a written instrument
containing the name of the donor and the name of the payee and drawn from the
account of the donor or the intermediary, as defined in Section 84302.

"(d) The value of all in-kind contributions of one hundred dollars ($100)
or more shall be reported in writing to the recipient upon the request in writing of
the recipient."

Pursuant to subdivision (c), the Commission has advised that contributions by money order and
cashier's check are also prohibited. The Avila Advice Letter No. I-99-006 explained the prohibition
on cashier's checks.

"Prior to 1996, the Act permitted a contributor to make a contribution
using a money order or cashier's checks, provided that the written instrument
contained the names of the contributor and the recipient of the contribution.
(Former Section 84300(c), amended by Stats. 1996, ch. 898.) However, those
responsible for enforcing the Act discovered that money orders and cashier's
checks were frequently used as vehicles for laundering money into campaigns.
(See, e.g., Cedar Valley Holding Company (1995) FPPC No. 94/022 [a 57-count
money laundering case in which cashier's checks were used to mask the true
source of campaign contributions] . . .) Such instruments could be easily
purchased without the need for personal identification and could include the name
of anyone other than the person who purchased it as the contributor. Surrogate
contributors, often employees of the real contributor, would allow the use of their
name on the money order or cashier's check to give a false and illegal impression
that they made the contribution. (See Press Release from the Fair Political
Practices Commission, FPPC Chair Applauds State Senate Action Against Money
Laundering, May 31, 1996 . . .) In some cases, the employees of a contributor
were unaware that their names would be used in this manner."

In 1996, the Legislature passed Senate Bill 1841, a Commission sponsored bill, to remedy
the loophole by adding subdivision (c). Based on subdivision (c), a contribution made by a cashier's
check cannot be for an amount more than $99 either singularly or cumulatively. Consequently,
there is no method, absent a bank account, to make a contribution of more than $99 to the ballot
measure committee.

File No. A-17-004
Page No. 3

2. Could an individual, serving as an intermediary, use their own personal checking account
to give a donation for a business?

No. The ban on cash contributions still applies. The Act does not prohibit contributions
made through intermediaries, so long as both the actual contributor and the intermediary are fully
reported as required by Section 84302. Section 84302 provides:

"No person shall make a contribution on behalf of another, or while acting
as the intermediary or agent of another, without disclosing to the recipient of the
contribution both his own full name and street address, occupation, and the name
of his employer, if any, or his principal place of business if he is self-employed,
and the full name and street address, occupation, and the name of employer, if
any, or principal place of business if self employed, of the other person. The
recipient of the contribution shall include in his campaign statement the full name
and street address, occupation, and the name of the employer, if any, or the
principal place of business if self-employed, of both the intermediary and the
contributor."

Regulation 18432.5 clarifies:

"(a) For purposes of Government Code Sections 84302, a person, as
defined in Government Code Section 82047, is an intermediary for a contribution
if any of the following applies:

"(1) The recipient of the contribution would consider the person to be the
contributor without the disclosure of the identity of the true source of the
contribution.

"(2) The person is an intermediary pursuant to Regulation 18419.

"(b) Any person who qualifies as an intermediary for the making of a
contribution shall disclose to the recipient of the contribution both his or her own
full name and street address, occupation, and the name of his employer, if any, or
his or her principal place of business if he or she is self-employed, and the full
name and street address, occupation, and the name of employer, if any, or
principal place of business if self-employed, of the contributor.

"(c) The recipient of the contribution shall include in his or her campaign
statement the name of the intermediary and other information disclosed pursuant
to Government Code Section 84302 and subdivision (b) of this section, if the
recipient knows or has reason to know that a contribution is made by an
intermediary."

However, the prohibition in Section 84300 still applies to the contributor's original
contribution to the intermediary. Therefore, the actual contributor cannot give the intermediary
more than $99 in cash or by cashier's check.

File No. A-17-004
Page No. 4

"Pursuant to legislative history discussed above, the language 'drawn from the account of the donor or intermediary' was intended to allow a contributor to write a check of $100 or more directly from his or her account to the intended recipient, or to the account of an intermediary, who would deposit the check and write another check to the intended recipient. Nothing in the legislative history indicates that the amendment was intended to allow the contributor to give a money order or cashier's check to the intermediary. In fact, the specific purpose of the legislation was to prohibit the use of money orders and cashier's checks in the making of contributions. [Citations.] It would be inconsistent with the purpose of the legislation to allow a contributor to use a cashier's check or money order through an intermediary, when the contributor is prohibited from doing so directly." (Avila Advice Letter No. I-99-006.)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

[Regarding intermediaries:] However, the prohibition in Section 84300 still applies to the contributor's original contribution to the intermediary. Therefore, the actual contributor cannot give the intermediary more than $99 in cash or by cashier's check.

sections.question_synthetic: Can businesses without bank accounts (due to federal restrictions on marijuana-related commerce) make contributions over $99.99 to a ballot measure committee, and can they use intermediaries with personal checking accounts to circumvent this limitation?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Contributions of $100 or more must be made by written instrument drawn from the donor's or intermediary's account; cash, money orders, and cashier's checks are prohibited. Businesses without bank accounts cannot legally make contributions exceeding $99.99, and using an intermediary does not circumvent this restriction since the prohibition applies to the original contribution from the business to the intermediary.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear FACTS, ANALYSIS, and two numbered questions with conclusions. Minor OCR artifacts present (e.g., 'witholll', 'otheiwise', 'intermedia,y') but content is clear and complete. Signature block present indicating this is the final FPPC response.

QUESTION: 1. Can you advise if there is any other way that a business can legally give more than $99.99 without having a checking account?

2. Could an individual, serving as an intermediary, use their own personal checking account to give a donation for a business?

CONCLUSION: There is not [a legal way for a business to give more than $99.99 without a checking account]. The Act prohibits making or receiving cash contributions of one hundred dollars or more. [Contributions by money order and cashier's check are also prohibited.] Consequently, there is no method, absent a bank account, to make a contribution of more than $99 to the ballot measure committee. [Regarding intermediaries:] However, the prohibition in Section 84300 still applies to the contributor's original contribution to the intermediary. Therefore, the actual contributor cannot give the intermediary more than $99 in cash or by cashier's check.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that medical marijuana businesses without bank accounts cannot legally contribute more than $99.99 to a ballot measure committee, as the Political Reform Act prohibits cash contributions and non-bank instruments (money orders, cashier's checks) exceeding $99.99, and using intermediaries does not provide a workaround.
